LAB affect dough rheology (how it behaves) through multiple mechanisms: 1) Acidification effects on gluten - Lower pH changes protein charge interactions, which can tighten or change gluten behavior. - Over long fermentation, acids can contribute to weakening if combined with proteolysis. 2) Proteolysis (protein breakdown) - Flour contains protease enzymes; LAB can also contribute indirectly by acidifying the environment, which can activate certain proteases. - Controlled proteolysis can improve extensibility (dough stretches more easily). - Excessive proteolysis can make dough slack, sticky, and unable to hold shape. 3) Exopolysaccharides (EPS) - Some LAB produce EPS, which can increase viscosity and potentially improve crumb softness and moisture retention in some cases. 4) Enzyme interactions and starch - Acid can influence amylase activity and starch behavior, impacting sugar availability and browning. So LAB are not just “sour makers”; they can meaningfully alter dough handling and final texture.
Back to my starter: if I thicken it, should I also change feeding ratios? Or can I keep the same?

You can keep the same ratio by weight, but the hydration change itself changes fermentation dynamics, so you might need to adjust timing rather than ratio. Options: A) Keep ratio, change hydration - Example: keep 10g starter, add 25g water, add 35g flour (thicker). - Still a similar dilution, but fermentation may be slower and peak later because diffusion is reduced and the matrix is stiffer. B) Keep hydration, change ratio - If you want to keep it at 100% hydration for convenience, you can adjust ratio and temperature to get the activity you want. If your goal is to assess “is it alive and getting stronger,” thickening helps because it makes rise easier to see. Once it’s strong, you can choose the hydration you prefer for maintenance.
I tried putting it in the oven with the light on for a few hours before, and it got a dried crust on top. Is that a problem?

A dried crust can be a problem because it: - reduces oxygen exchange at the surface, - can trap a layer that doesn’t ferment and may harbor different microbes, - and makes it harder to judge activity. To prevent crusting: - Use a lid (not airtight if you’re worried about pressure, but in practice a loosely screwed lid works well). - Or cover with plastic wrap with a small vent. - Keep humidity higher in the oven by placing a mug of warm water nearby. A little surface drying isn’t catastrophic; you can remove the crust and continue. But it’s better to prevent it so the starter stays uniform and you get reliable readings.
Is it okay to keep the lid on tight? Won’t it explode?

In a small starter jar, pressure buildup is usually modest, but a fully sealed container can still be risky if left long enough, especially if the starter becomes very active. Safer approach: - Use a lid screwed on loosely, or - Use a jar with a lid that can vent slightly, or - Use a breathable cover (cloth) if drying isn’t an issue. If you do use a tight lid, make sure there’s headspace and you check it regularly. But generally, “loosely covered” is the standard compromise: prevents drying and contamination, allows minimal venting.
I’m going to try your 10g:25g:25g with filtered water and 10% rye, and I’ll use a narrower jar. If it starts rising, when do I declare it “ready” to bake with?

A practical readiness checklist: 1) Predictable rise - It at least doubles (or shows strong expansion) at a known ratio and temperature, consistently for 2–3 cycles. 2) Reasonable time-to-peak - For example, at ~25°C, a mature-ish starter might double at 1:2:2 within ~4–8 hours. There’s variation, but you want it not to take 20+ hours. 3) Aroma is clean and pleasant - Bready, mildly tangy, maybe fruity; not rotten, putrid, or strongly solvent. 4) Texture at peak is aerated - Lots of bubbles throughout, spongy. If you meet those, you can bake. Even a starter that’s not “perfect” can leaven bread if you adjust fermentation time, but having it predictable makes everything easier.
While we wait for that, can you explain why some starters smell like acetone?

Acetone-like or solvent/nail-polish aromas often come from volatile compounds like ethyl acetate (fruity-solvent) and other metabolites produced under stress or starvation. Common causes: - Underfeeding: the culture runs out of readily available sugars, and metabolism shifts. Yeasts may produce more ethanol; bacteria and yeasts can produce esters. - Very long fermentation at warm temps: waste products accumulate. - High inoculation + long interval: lots of microbes, limited food, quick depletion. It’s not necessarily “bad” in the sense of unsafe; it’s a sign the culture is hungry or imbalanced. Usually, a few regular feeds at appropriate ratios (and possibly cooler temps if it’s racing) will bring it back to a more bready aroma.
Does that relate to the “hooch” layer?

Yes, often. Hooch is a liquid layer that can form when the starter has fermented past peak and begins separating. It can contain: - water, - ethanol (from yeast), - acids and other metabolites. It’s a sign the starter is underfed or left too long between feeds. You can stir it back in (for more sourness) or pour it off (to reduce harshness), then feed. In your case, no hooch suggests it’s not overfermenting; it’s more like it’s not ramping up much at all.
What role do enzymes in the flour play? Are they from the wheat, or from microbes?

Both, but flour’s own enzymes are the main source initially. Key flour enzymes: 1) Amylases (alpha and beta) - Break starch into smaller sugars (maltose, glucose). - Essential because yeast and LAB need fermentable sugars; flour doesn’t start with much free sugar. 2) Proteases - Break down proteins into peptides and amino acids. - Provide nitrogen sources for microbes and affect dough structure. 3) Lipases and others - Minor roles in flavor development and nutrient availability. Microbes also produce enzymes, but in a starter, the flour’s endogenous enzymes are crucial because they continuously generate sugars from starch, fueling fermentation over time. Enzyme activity depends on: - flour type (whole grain vs refined), - malted additions, - temperature, - pH (many enzymes have pH optima), - and time.
If amylases create sugars, why would a starter ever “run out of food”?

Because the rate of sugar generation and the rate of sugar consumption can diverge. Even though starch is abundant, fermentable sugars are produced at a finite rate. If microbial activity is high, they can consume sugars faster than enzymes release them, especially in a warm, active starter with a lot of inoculum. Also: - As pH changes, enzyme activity can change. - Some sugars become less available depending on viscosity and diffusion. - Microbes may preferentially consume certain sugars; when those are depleted, metabolism shifts. So “running out of food” usually means “running out of readily fermentable sugars at the rate microbes want,” not literal depletion of starch.
I’ve read that sourdough LAB like maltose and yeast can’t use maltose as well, so they don’t compete. Is that true?

There’s some truth in that generalization, though it depends on species. - Many sourdough-associated LAB are good at utilizing maltose, a major sugar produced from starch by amylases. - Some sourdough yeasts are less competitive for maltose or preferentially use glucose/fructose, which can reduce direct competition and allow coexistence. This division of labor can stabilize the ecosystem: - LAB consume maltose and produce acids. - Yeasts consume other sugars and produce CO₂ and ethanol. - LAB can also influence sugar availability and the environment in ways that indirectly support yeast. However, it’s not a universal rule. Some yeasts can use maltose well. The actual balance depends on the exact strains present.
If I wanted more sour flavor in bread, would I tune the starter to be more acetic? Or is it more about dough fermentation?

More about dough fermentation, though starter maintenance can influence it. To push sourness (especially acetic/vinegary notes) in the final bread, typical levers are: - Lower fermentation temperature (slower, favors acetic character), - Longer fermentation (more acid accumulation), - Stiffer preferments (often more acetic), - Lower inoculation + longer time (gives LAB more time to produce acids), - Retardation (cold proofing) to build flavor. Starter tuning: - A stiffer, cooler-maintained starter can skew its acid profile, but once it’s mixed into dough, the dough conditions dominate. - Also, too much acidity in the starter can weaken dough if used in high proportion, so it’s a balance. So you can adjust starter maintenance a bit, but the main flavor control is how you ferment the dough.

Update: I filtered water, added 10% rye, did 10g starter + 25g water + 25g flour, and put it in the oven with the light on. After 8 hours it rose maybe 30% and has more bubbles. Smell is more bready, less green apple.

That’s a meaningful improvement—more bubbles plus a cleaner, bready aroma suggests the community is shifting in the right direction and yeast are likely increasing. At this point, the main question is whether it continues to accelerate over the next couple of feeds. What I’d do next: 1) Wait for a clearer peak - If it’s still rising at 8 hours, let it go until it either stops rising or begins to flatten. Note the peak time. 2) Feed based on behavior - If it peaks around, say, 10–14 hours, you can feed then. - If it’s still not peaking by 24 hours, feed at 24 hours anyway to avoid too much acid accumulation. 3) Keep conditions stable - Same ratio, same temp, same flour mix for at least 2–3 cycles so you can see the trend. If it goes from 30% rise to doubling within a couple days under the same conditions, you’re basically there.
It peaked around 14 hours, maybe 50% rise, then started to flatten. Should I feed it right at flattening, or wait until it collapses?

Feed it around peak to just-after-peak—when it’s flattening is a good moment. Reasons: - Yeast and LAB populations are high. - The matrix hasn’t broken down too much yet. - You avoid carrying over a lot of harsh acidity and degraded structure. So yes: feed at flattening. If you want to push it to become faster, you can shorten the interval slightly by feeding at peak/early decline consistently. You can keep the same ratio for now. If it starts peaking faster (e.g., 8–10 hours), you can either: - switch to twice-daily feeds at the same ratio, or - increase the feeding ratio (e.g., 1:3:3) to give it more food per cycle.
I fed it again, same ratio. This time it rose about 90% in 10 hours. Is that basically doubling? It’s close.

That’s close enough to call it “nearly doubling,” and the acceleration (50% in 14 hours → ~90% in 10 hours) is exactly what you want to see. It indicates increasing microbial density and improved performance under your conditions. If the next feed hits a true double within a similar or shorter time window, you can consider it ready to leaven dough. To make your “doubling” easier to interpret: - Use a rubber band and mark the initial level precisely. - Choose a jar where the starter column is narrow; small absolute expansion becomes a clear percentage. If you want a little extra push, you can keep the temperature steady and do one more cycle at the same ratio.
Great. Now I’m curious: why does it speed up so quickly once it starts working? Is that just population growth?

Mostly population growth plus selection. Two reinforcing processes: 1) Population increases - Each successful cycle increases the number of viable cells you carry into the next feeding (even though you discard most, the remaining inoculum is healthier and denser). - With more cells at the start, the lag phase shortens and CO₂ production ramps sooner. 2) Selection for organisms adapted to your regime - Your feeding ratio, flour mix, hydration, and temperature are a selective environment. - Microbes that grow fastest under those exact conditions become more dominant over repeated cycles. Also, as the ecosystem stabilizes: - pH becomes more consistently in the sourdough range. - Competing or inhibitory early-stage microbes are suppressed. - Yeast-LAB interactions become more balanced. That’s why starters often feel “stuck” and then suddenly become active over a couple of days.
If I want to keep it at room temp long-term (20–21°C), will it stay strong?

It can, but it will likely run on a slower schedule. At 20–21°C: - It may take longer to peak after feeding. - You might find that once-daily feeding works fine, but the ratio may need adjustment (often a lower inoculation or higher ratio depending on how fast it moves). Maintenance options at 20–21°C: A) Once daily, moderate ratio - Example: 1:2:2 or 1:3:3, depending on how fast it peaks. - Feed when it’s near peak/early decline, which might be ~12–24 hours. B) Keep it cooler/warmer intentionally - If you can’t, just accept the slower rhythm. C) Use a small amount of whole grain occasionally - Even 5–10% rye once in a while can keep it lively. The key is matching feeding interval to its peak time at that temperature. If you feed too early at cool temps, you keep it diluted and it can feel weak again.
I know you said no salt, but what about adding a tiny bit of honey or sugar to “feed” it?

It’s usually unnecessary and can sometimes skew the ecology. Why adding sugar isn’t typically recommended: - Flour already provides a sustained sugar supply through enzymatic starch breakdown. - Adding simple sugars can favor fast sugar-fermenters and potentially reduce the selective advantage of sourdough-adapted microbes that thrive on flour-derived sugars (like maltose). - It can also change osmotic pressure slightly and affect fermentation profile. If a starter is truly struggling, a tiny amount of sugar might produce visible bubbles, but it’s more like a diagnostic stimulant than a solution. Since your starter is improving on flour alone, I’d keep it flour-based to build the right long-term community.
How do I avoid making it too acidic if I’m feeding once daily at cooler temps?

Two main strategies: 1) Increase the feeding ratio (more fresh flour/water per amount of starter) - If you notice it peaks early and then sits acidic for many hours, increase ratio. - Example: move from 1:1:1 to 1:2:2 or 1:3:3. - That dilutes acids and provides more substrate, lengthening the cycle. 2) Reduce inoculation (keep less starter) - Similar effect: less carryover acidity and more “runway” before it becomes overly acidic. Signs it’s too acidic/overfermented between feeds: - Strong sharp vinegar/solvent notes. - Starter becomes very runny over time. - It rises then collapses and stays collapsed for many hours before feeding time. At 20–21°C, you may find a ratio like 1:3:3 once daily keeps it in a better balance than 1:1:1.
When I bake, should I use starter at peak, or can I use it after it’s fallen?

Peak (or just before peak) is the most reliable for leavening power because yeast activity and gas retention are high and the culture is not overly acidic or depleted. Using it after it’s fallen: - It still contains microbes, but yeast may be less active and the starter may be more acidic. - Dough fermentation may be slower and flavor may skew more sour. - It can work if you adjust timing, but it’s less predictable. A good compromise is to use it at peak or within an hour or two after peak. If you can’t, you can do a small “levain build” timed to peak when you want to mix dough.
Levain build vs starter—what’s the difference?

Functionally, a levain is a build of starter made specifically for a bake, often at a particular ratio, flour type, and hydration to achieve desired timing and flavor. Starter: - Your maintained culture, kept going indefinitely. Levain: - A one-off (or occasional) preferment made from a small inoculation of starter. - Often built larger so you have enough for the recipe without depleting your maintenance starter. - Can be tuned: stiff/loose, warm/cool, white/whole grain, etc. Microbiologically it’s the same community; the levain is just that community grown under specific conditions for a specific purpose.
If I want an open crumb, does starter health matter, or is it mostly shaping and hydration?

Starter health matters, but open crumb is multi-factor. Starter health contributes: - Adequate gas production and fermentation timing. - Predictable dough strength changes over time. But open crumb is also strongly influenced by: - Dough hydration (higher generally supports openness, but must be matched to flour strength). - Gluten development (mixing, folds). - Fermentation degree (underproofed vs overproofed). - Shaping technique (preserving gas). - Flour quality and protein. - Baking setup (steam, heat). A weak starter can lead to underfermented dough or uneven fermentation, which often gives tight crumb. A strong starter doesn’t guarantee open crumb, but it makes it possible to hit the right fermentation window.
Okay, another update: third feed, it doubled in about 7 hours at the warm temp. That seems good. Smell is pleasantly tangy, like yogurt and bread.

That’s solid performance. Doubling in ~7 hours at your ratio and warm temp suggests the starter is now robust enough to bake with. If you want to transition back to your normal routine: - Decide whether you’ll keep maintaining warm (faster) or at room temp (slower). - If you want to reduce rye, taper it down over a couple feeds. If you plan to bake soon, you can: - Feed it so that it peaks when you want to mix your dough, or - Build a levain timed to peak. If you tell me your typical bake schedule (when you want to mix), I can suggest a simple levain build timing.
Let’s say I want to mix dough tomorrow morning around 9am. It’s 7pm now. Room temp overnight is 20°C. How would you time a levain build?

You want a levain that peaks around 9am at ~20°C overnight. At that cooler temperature, fermentation is slower, so you can use a smaller inoculation and/or a higher feeding ratio. A straightforward overnight levain: - At 7–8pm: take 10g active starter - Add 40g water - Add 40g flour That’s 1:4:4 at 100% hydration. At 20°C, many starters will peak in roughly 10–14 hours at that ratio, which lines up with 9am. Since your starter is doubling in 7 hours at warm temp at ~1:2.5:2.5, 1:4:4 at 20°C is a reasonable starting point. To increase confidence: - Make it slightly stiffer (e.g., 10g starter + 40g water + 50g flour) if you want it to hold a peak longer and be less likely to overrun. - Use mostly bread flour; a small amount of rye (5–10%) is fine but may speed it up. If you can keep it slightly warmer than 20°C overnight (even 22°C), it will peak earlier; in that case, increase ratio to 1:5:5 or use cooler water.
If it peaks before I wake up, is it ruined?

Not ruined. It just might be past peak and a bit more acidic, with slightly reduced leavening power. What you can do if it peaked early: - Use it anyway and expect fermentation to be a bit slower or flavor a bit tangier. - Or do a quick “refresh” in the morning: take a small portion (e.g., 20g), feed 1:1:1 or 1:2:2, keep warm, and wait a few hours—this is basically a short second build. To avoid early peak: - Use a higher ratio (more flour/water per starter), - Use cooler water in the build, - Keep it in a cooler spot overnight.
One last microbiology question: is sourdough starter “stable” in the sense that it reaches an equilibrium community, or does it keep changing?

It reaches a relatively stable dynamic equilibrium under consistent conditions, but it’s not static. Stable in practice means: - Similar performance cycle to cycle, - Similar aroma/flavor profile, - Similar timing to peak, - Similar acid balance. But it can drift because: - Changing flour types changes nutrients and inoculum. - Temperature shifts select for different organisms. - Feeding ratios and intervals change selection pressure. - Even small differences in hydration and oxygen exposure can matter. So it’s stable like a garden is stable: you can maintain a consistent ecosystem, but it responds to how you care for it.
This has been extremely helpful. I’ll do the overnight levain. If I run into issues with the dough, I’ll ask.

Sounds good. If you end up troubleshooting dough, the most useful details will be: flour type, hydration, salt %, inoculation (how much levain), dough temperature, bulk fermentation time/shape of rise, and proofing conditions. That lets us connect the microbial activity to the dough’s physical behavior and adjust scientifically.
